text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Emily Hart  
St George’s University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Emily Hart,  

Michael Carter, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: H392847 NHS No. 9012745632  
Flat 5, 28 Clapham Park Road, London SW4 7BN 

Diagnoses: 

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (both generalised and focal seizure types) with emerging new event phenotype. MRI brain (2017) non-lesional. EEG (2023) mixed findings with intermittent generalised spike-wave and independent left temporal sharp waves. 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1 g bd  
Lamotrigine 150 mg bd  
Clobazam 10 mg nocte (intermittent daytime 5 mg as rescue on high-risk days by patient report) 

Plan: 

- Urgent ambulatory EEG with video capture to characterise new events and correlate with diary.  
- Increase Lamotrigine to 175 mg bd (subject to tolerability and bloods) and consider rationalisation of clobazam depending on EEG correlation.  
- Safety counselling reinforced regarding bathing, heights, cooking, and SUDEP risk; car driving remains contraindicated.  
- Bloods today: FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, vitamin D; check antiepileptic levels where applicable.  
- Refer to epilepsy specialist nurse for rescue plan review and to falls clinic re daily drop attacks.  
- Consider surgical workup if focal onset to generalisation is confirmed (repeat MRI with epilepsy protocol and MDT discussion).  
- Review in clinic in 8–10 weeks; earlier if events escalate or injuries occur. 

I reviewed Michael Carter in my clinic today following concerns about a deterioration in his epilepsy, specifically the emergence of a new event phenotype compared with his prior descriptions. He described episodes over the past three months featuring abrupt behavioural arrest with bilateral arm stiffening and a forward lurch, sometimes preceded by a brief rising epigastric sensation and followed by several minutes of confusion; these differ from his usual focal impaired-awareness spells and his established generalised tonic-clonic seizures. His partner has captured two events on a smartphone video, which suggest sudden atonic elements with potential focal evolution; we will seek objective characterisation with ambulatory EEG and video correlation. 

Seizure frequency remains unchanged over the last six months; he continues to have up to 3 generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, rarely achieving more than ten consecutive seizure-free days. He also has daily drop attacks. Furthermore, focal impaired-awareness seizures with disorientation are reported every four to six weeks. No events have occurred since his most recent review. While the overall count is stable, the qualitative change in semiology and the daily drop attacks indicate functional deterioration and increased risk of injury. He reports two minor head impacts in the kitchen without loss of consciousness and increased postictal fatigue. Sleep is fragmented by early-morning awakenings; alcohol intake is minimal; adherence is good by his account and blister-pack records. 

His background is of combined generalised and focal epilepsy, with both generalised and focal seizure types documented historically. Prior EEGs have shown generalised spike-wave discharges as well as left temporal sharp waves, and his MRI has been non-lesional. We discussed that the appearance of new atonic-like events may represent evolution of his epilepsy or medication-related lowering of threshold for drop attacks, though the latter is less likely given stable dosing. Given the injury risk, I recommend proceeding with ambulatory EEG urgently and modestly up-titrating lamotrigine while monitoring for rash, dizziness, or diplopia. If the new events are confirmed as focal onset with rapid generalisation or as true atonic seizures, we will revisit broad treatment options including optimisation of current regimen, considering sodium channel agent substitution, or referral for surgical/neuromodulation evaluation (VNS/DBS) after MDT discussion. 

I have reinforced safety precautions and provided written information on seizure first aid. The epilepsy specialist nurse will update his rescue plan and explore protective headwear for high-risk activities. I will write with results and see him back in 8–10 weeks, sooner if there is any further deterioration or significant injury. 

With best wishes,

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
Identify explicit frequency statements:
- "he continues to have up to 3 generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year" → 3 per year.
- "rarely achieving more than ten consecutive seizure-free days" suggests frequent events but not a precise rate.
- "He also has daily drop attacks" → daily = 1 or more per day; explicit and highest frequency.
- "focal impaired-awareness seizures ... every four to six weeks" → approximately 1 per month.
Rule: choose the highest frequency among multiple seizure types. Daily drop attacks represent the highest frequency. Therefore, final label is 1 per day (daily).
seizure_frequency_number: He also has daily drop attacks.
reference: Seizure frequency remains unchanged over the last six months; he continues to have up to 3 generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, rarely achieving more than ten consecutive seizure-free days. He also has daily drop attacks. Furthermore, focal impaired-awareness seizures with disorientation are reported every four to six weeks. No events have occurred since his most recent review
